title: Patterns with numbers
description: Generate terms in a pattern when given a rule.  Identify features of a pattern that are not explicit to the rule itself.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows a list of numbers. For every number in the table, the rule is to multiply it by $3$.
   
**Fill in the table below to match the pattern.**

Number | Multiply by 3
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $3$
$2$ | $6$
$3$ | [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
$4$ | $12$
$5$ | [[Ōśā numeric-input 2]]
$6$ | [[Ōśā numeric-input 3]]
hints:
The pattern that connects each pair of numbers is to multiply by $\greenD3$.

Number | |Multiply by $3$
:-: | :-:| :-: 
$1$ | $\greenD{{}\times3}$|$3$
$2$ | $\greenD{{}\times3}$| $6$
$3$ | $\greenD{{}\times3}$| $\blueD?$
$4$ | $\greenD{{}\times3}$| $12$
$5$ | $\greenD{{}\times3}$| $\blueD?$
$6$ |$\greenD{{}\times3}$| $\blueD?$

Here is the completed table:  

Number | Multiply by 3
:-: | :-:
$1$ | $3$
$2$ | $6$
$3$ | $9$
$4$ | $12$
$5$ | $15$
$6$ | $18$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows a list of numbers. For every number in the table, the rule is to add $8$.
   
**Fill in the table below to match the pattern.**

Number | Add $8$
:-: | :-:
$4$ | [[Ōśā numeric-input 1]]
$10$ | $18$
$22$ | [[Ōśā numeric-input 2]]
$54$ | $62$
$100$ |[[Ōśā numeric-input 3]]
hints:
The pattern that connects each pair of numbers is to add $\greenD8$.

Number | |Add $8$
:-: | :-:| :-: 
$4$ | $\greenD{{}+8}$| $\blueD?$
$10$ | $\greenD{{}+8}$| $18$
$22$ | $\greenD{{}+8}$| $\blueD?$
$54$ | $\greenD{{}+8}$| $62$
$100$ | $\greenD{{}+8}$| $\blueD?$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the number of legs on multiple spiders.

Spiders | Number of Legs
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$2$ |$16$
$3$ |$24$
$4$ |$32$
$5$ |$40$

**If the pattern continues, how many legs will $9$ spiders have?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
The pattern that connects each pair of numbers is to multiply by $\greenD8$.

Number | |Multiply by 8
:-: | :-:| :-: 
$2$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$|$16$
$3$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $24$
$4$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $32$
$5$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $40$
If we want to know how many legs $9$ spiders have, we need to multiply $9\greenD{{}\times8}$. 

Number | |Multiply by 8
:-: | :-:| :-: 
$2$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$|$16$
$3$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $24$
$4$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $32$
$5$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $40$
$...$ | $...$| $...$
$9$ | $\greenD{{}\times8}$| $\blueD{72}$

There will be $\blueD{72}$ legs on $9$ spiders.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the number of minutes it takes to run laps.

Laps | Minutes
:-: | :-:
$6$ | $30$
$7$ | $35$
$8$ | $40$
$9$ | $45$
$10$ | $50$

**If the pattern continues, how many minutes will it take to run $13$ laps?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
The pattern that connects each pair of numbers is to multiply by $\greenD5$.

Number | |Multiply by 5
:-: | :-:| :-: 
$6$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$|$30$
$7$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $35$
$8$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $40$
$9$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $45$
$10$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $50$

If we want to know how many minutes it will take to run $13$ laps, we need to multiply $13\greenD{{}\times5}$. 

Number | |Multiply by 5
:-: | :-:| :-: 
$6$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$|$30$
$7$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $35$
$8$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $40$
$9$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $45$
$10$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $50$
$...$ | $...$| $...$
$13$ | $\greenD{{}\times5}$| $\blueD{65}$
It will take $\blueD{65}$ minutes to run $13$ laps.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kira and Sam were born on the same day but not in the same year. Their ages are shown on the table below. 


||||||
- | -  | - | - | - | - 
**Kira's age** | $9$ | $12$ | $16$ | $24$| $30$
**Sam's age** | $12$ | $15$ | $19$ | $27$ | $33$

**How old will Sam be when Kira is $36$?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
The pattern that connects each pair of numbers is to add $\greenD3$.

||||||
- | -  | - | - | - | - 
**Kira's age** | $9$ | $12$ | $16$ | $24$| $30$
| $\greenD{+3}$ | $\greenD{+3}$ | $\greenD{+3}$ | $\greenD{+3}$ | $\greenD{+3}$ 
**Sam's age** | $12$ | $15$ | $19$ | $27$ | $33$
Sam will be $\blueD{39}$ years old.

assessment_items.question_content:
The first day of Sam's vacation was a Monday. 

**What was the day of the week on Sam's $36^{\text{th}}$ day of vacation?**
  
Day $36$ of Sam's vacation was a [[Ōśā dropdown 1]].
hints: Every $7$ days, the days of the week repeat.

Day number | Day of the week
:-: | :-:
$\maroonD{1}$ | $\maroonD{\text{Monday}}$
$2$ | Tuesday
$3$ | Wednesday
$4$ | Thursday
$5$ | Friday
$6$ | Saturday
$\blueE{7}$ | $\blueE{\text{Sunday}}$
$\maroonD{8}$ | $\maroonD{\text{Monday}}$
$9$ | Tuesday
**...** | **...**
$36$ | $\purpleD{?}$

It will be faster to count by *weeks* than days.

Week number | Day number | Day of the week
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $7$ | Sunday
$2$ | $14$ | Sunday
$3$ | $21$ | Sunday
$4$ | $28$ | Sunday
$5$ | $35$ | Sunday

It will take $1$ more day to get to day $36$.

The $35^{\text{th}}$ day is Sunday, so the $36^{\text{th}}$ day is Monday.  
  
We could use division to find the same information.

$\overset{\text{total days}}{36}\div\underset{\text{days per week}}{7}=\overset{\text{full weeks}}{5}\,\text{remainder}\,\underset{\text{extra day}}{1}$
The $36^{\text{th}}$ day of Sam's vacation was a Monday.

assessment_items.question_content:
The first day of Alex's trip was a Monday. 

**What was the day of the week on the $39^{\text{th}}$ day of his trip?**

Day $39$ of Alex's trip was a [[Ōśā dropdown 1]].
hints: Every $7$ days, the days of the week repeat.

Day number | Day of the week
:-: | :-:
$\maroonD{1}$ | $\maroonD{\text{Monday}}$
$2$ | Tuesday
$3$ | Wednesday
$4$ | Thursday
$5$ | Friday
$6$ | Saturday
$\blueE{7}$ | $\blueE{\text{Sunday}}$
$\maroonD{8}$ | $\maroonD{\text{Monday}}$
$9$ | Tuesday
**...** | **...**
$39$ | $\purpleD{?}$

It will be faster to count by *weeks* than days.

Week number | Day number | Day of the week
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $7$ | Sunday
$2$ | $14$ | Sunday
$3$ | $21$ | Sunday
$4$ | $28$ | Sunday
$5$ | $35$ | Sunday

It will take $4$ more days to get to day $39$.

The $35^{\text{th}}$ day is Sunday, so the $39^{\text{th}}$ day is Thursday.  
  
We could use division to find the same information.

$\overset{\text{total days}}{39}\div\underset{\text{days per week}}{7}=\overset{\text{full weeks}}{5}\,\text{remainder}\,\underset{\text{extra days}}{4}$
The $39^\text{th}$ day of Alex's trip was a Thursday.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pablo Picasso is making one-color versions of all his paintings. He uses the colors in the following order and then starts over from the beginning: $\redD{\text{red}}$, $\blueD{\text{blue}}$, $\maroonD{\text{pink}}$, $\greenD{\text{green}}$, and $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$.  

**What color is painting $27$ ?**

The color of painting $27$ is [[Ōśā dropdown 1]].
hints: Every $5$ paintings, the colors repeat.

Painting number | Color
:-: | :-:
$1$ | red
$2$ | blue
$3$ | pink
$4$ | green
$5$ | $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$
$6$ | red
$7$ | blue
$8$ | pink
$9$ | green
$10$ | $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$
$\ldots$ | $\ldots$
$27$ | $?$
  
It will be faster to count by *groups of $5$* than by individual paintings.

Group number | Painting number | Color
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $5$ | purple
$2$ | $10$ | purple
$3$ | $15$ | purple
$4$ | $20$ | purple
$5$ | $25$ | purple

It will take $2$ more paintings to get to painting $27$.

We could use division to find the same information.

$\overset{\text{total paintings}}{27}\div\underset{\text{paintings per group}}{5}=\overset{\text{full groups}}{5}\,\text{remainder}\,\underset{\text{extra paintings}}{2}$

Painting number | Color
:-: | :-:
$25$ | $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$
$26$ | red
$27$ | $\blueD{\text{blue}}$
  
The $25^{\text{th}}$ painting is purple, so the $27^{\text{th}}$ painting is blue.
The color of the $27^{\text{th}}$ painting is $\blueD{\text{blue}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vincent van Gogh is making one-color versions of all his paintings. He uses the colors in the following order and then starts over from the beginning: $\redD{\text{red}}$, $\blueD{\text{blue}}$, $\maroonD{\text{pink}}$, $\greenD{\text{green}}$, and $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$.

**What color is painting $31$ ?**

The color of painting $31$ is [[Ōśā dropdown 1]].
hints: Every $5$ paintings, the colors repeat.

Painting number | Color
:-: | :-:
$1$ | red
$2$ | blue
$3$ | pink
$4$ | green
$5$ | $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$
$6$ | red
$7$ | blue
$8$ | pink
$9$ | green
$10$ | $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$
$\ldots$ | $\ldots$
$31$ | $?$
  
It will be faster to count by *groups of $5$* than by individual paintings.

Group number | Painting number | Color
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $5$ | purple
$2$ | $10$ | purple
$3$ | $15$ | purple
$4$ | $20$ | purple
$5$ | $25$ | purple
$6$ | $30$ | purple

It will take $1$ more painting to get to painting $31$.

We could use division to find the same information.

$\overset{\text{total paintings}}{31}\div\underset{\text{paintings per group}}{5}=\overset{\text{full groups}}{6}\,\text{remainder}\,\underset{\text{extra painting}}{1}$

Painting number | Color
:-: | :-:
$30$ | $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$
$31$ | $\redD{\text{red}}$
  
The $30^{\text{th}}$ painting is purple, so the $31^{\text{th}}$ painting is red.
The color of the $31^{\text{st}}$ painting is $\redD{\text{red}}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dorothy gives a ribbon to each of her friends in the following order and then starts over from the beginning: the Scarecrow, the Tinman, the Cowardly Lion, and Toto.  

**Who gets the $18^\text{th}$ ribbon?**
  
[[Ōśā dropdown 1]] gets the $18^\text{th}$ ribbon.
hints: Every $4$ ribbons, the friends repeat.

Ribbon number | Friend who gets it
:-: | :-:
$1$ | Scarecrow
$2$ | Tinman
$3$ | Cowardly Lion
$4$ | $\purpleD{\text{Toto}}$
$5$ | Scarecrow
$6$ | Tinman
$7$ | Cowardly Lion
$8$ | $\purpleD{\text{Toto}}$
$\ldots$ | $\ldots$
$18$ | $?$
  
It will be faster to count by *groups of $4$* than by individual ribbons.

Group number | Ribbon number | Friend
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $4$ | Toto
$2$ | $8$ | Toto
$3$ | $12$ | Toto
$4$ | $16$ | Toto

It will take $2$ more ribbons to get to ribbon $18$.

We could use division to find the same information.

$\overset{\text{total ribbons}}{18}\div\underset{\text{ribbons per group}}{4}=\overset{\text{full groups}}{4}\,\text{remainder}\,\underset{\text{extra ribbons}}{2}$

Ribbon number | Friend who gets it
:-: | :-:
$16$ | $\purpleD{\text{Toto}}$
$17$ | Scarecrow
$18$ | Tinman

Toto gets the  $16^{\text{th}}$ ribbon, so the Tinman gets the $18^{\text{th}}$ ribbon.
The Tinman gets the $18^{\text{th}}$ ribbon.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dorothy gives a cherry to each of her friends in the following order and then starts over from the beginning: the Scarecrow, the Tinman, the Cowardly Lion, and Toto.

**Who gets the $23^\text{rd}$ cherry?**

[[Ōśā dropdown 1]] gets the $23^\text{rd}$ cherry.
hints: Every $4$ cherries, the friends repeat.

Cherry number | Friend who gets it
:-: | :-:
$1$ | Scarecrow
$2$ | Tinman
$3$ | Cowardly Lion
$4$ | $\purpleD{\text{Toto}}$
$5$ | Scarecrow
$6$ | Tinman
$7$ | Cowardly Lion
$8$ | $\purpleD{\text{Toto}}$
$\ldots$ | $\ldots$
$23$ | $?$
  
It will be faster to count by *groups of $4$* than by individual cherries.

Group number | Cherry number | Friend
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $4$ | Toto
$2$ | $8$ | Toto
$3$ | $12$ | Toto
$4$ | $16$ | Toto
$5$ | $20$ | Toto

It will take $3$ more cherries to get to cherry $23$.

We could use division to find the same information.

$\overset{\text{total cherries}}{23}\div\underset{\text{cherries per group}}{4}=\overset{\text{full groups}}{5}\,\text{remainder}\,\underset{\text{extra cherries}}{3}$

Cherry number | Friend who gets it
:-: | :-:
$20$ | $\purpleD{\text{Toto}}$
$21$ | Scarecrow
$22$ | Tinman
$23$ | Cowardly Lion

Toto gets the  $20^{\text{th}}$ cherry, so the Cowardly Lion gets the $23^{\text{rd}}$ cherry.
The Cowardly Lion gets the $23^{\text{rd}}$ cherry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the pattern shown?**

$1, 6, 36, 216$ 

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's see if we can find the pattern that connects each number.

$1\overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}6\overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 36 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 216$

The pattern is to start at $1$ and multiply by $6$ each time.

$1\overset{\blueD{\times6}}{{\rightarrow}}6\overset{\blueD{\times6}}{\rightarrow} 36 \overset{\blueD{\times6}}{\rightarrow} 216$
The rule for the pattern $1, 6, 36, 216$   is $\blueD{\text{multiply by } 6}$ each time.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the pattern shown?**

$4, 16, 64, 256, 1024$ 

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's see if we can find the pattern that connects each number.

$4\overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}16\overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 64 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 256 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 1024$

The pattern is to start at $1$ and multiply by $4$ each time.

$4\overset{\blueD{\times~4}}{{\rightarrow}}16\overset{\blueD{\times~4}}{\rightarrow} 64 \overset{\blueD{\times~4}}{\rightarrow} 256 \overset{\blueD{\times~4}}{\rightarrow} 1024$
The rule for the pattern $4, 16, 64, 256, 1024$   is $\blueD{\text{multiply by } 4}$ each time.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the pattern shown?**

$41, 54, 67, 80, 93, 106$ 

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's see if we can find the pattern that connects each number.

$41\overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}54\overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 67 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 80 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 93 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 106$

The pattern is to start at $41$ and add $13$ each time.

$41\overset{\blueD{+~13}}{{\rightarrow}}54\overset{\blueD{+~13}}{\rightarrow} 67 \overset{\blueD{+~13}}{\rightarrow} 80 \overset{\blueD{+~13}}{\rightarrow} 93 \overset{\blueD{+~13}}{\rightarrow} 106$
The rule for the pattern $41, 54, 67, 80, 93, 106$   is $\blueD{\text{add } 13}$ each time.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rule describes the pattern shown?**

$5, 32, 59, 86, 113, 140$ 

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's see if we can find the pattern that connects each number.

$5\overset{\blueD?}{{\rightarrow}}32\overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 59 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 86 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 113 \overset{\blueD?}{\rightarrow} 140$

The pattern is to start at $5$ and add $27$ each time.

$5\overset{\blueD{+~27}}{{\rightarrow}}32\overset{\blueD{+~27}}{\rightarrow} 59 \overset{\blueD{+~27}}{\rightarrow} 86 \overset{\blueD{+~27}}{\rightarrow} 113 \overset{\blueD{+~27}}{\rightarrow} 140$
The rule for the pattern $5, 32, 59, 86, 113, 140$    is $\blueD{\text{add } 27}$ each time.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following pattern starts with $5$ and uses the rule *multiply the previous term by $3$.*

$5, 15, 45, 135, 405$

**Which of the following statements is true?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's see if the sum of each number's digits is $\green9$.

Number| Sum of digits  
- | - | -
$5$ | $\red5$
$15$ | $1 + 5 = \red6$
$45$ | $4 + 5 = \green9$
$135$ | $1 + 3+ 5 = \green9$
$405$ | $4 + 0 + 5 = \green9$

Not every number's digits have a sum of $\green9$.

Multiples of $3$ are numbers that can be a product of $3$ and another whole number. 

$3 \times \, ? = 5$

There is no whole number that can be multiplied by $3$ to get a product of $5$.  So, $5$ is not a multiple of $3$.

$\qquad \blueD5, 1\blueD5, 4\blueD5, 13\blueD5, 40\blueD5$

Every number has a $\blueD {5 \text{ in the ones place}}$.

The only true statement is:

- Every number has a $5$ in the ones place.

choices.content: The sum of each number's digits  is $9$.
The pattern includes only multiples of $3$.
Every number has a $5$ in the ones place.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following pattern starts with $4$ and uses the rule *add $6$ to the previous term.*

$4, 10, 16, 22, 28$

**Which of the following statements is true?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
even number $+$ even number $=$ even number

We start with an even number $(4)$ and keep adding even numbers $(6)$. So, every number in this pattern is even.  There are no odd numbers.

Multiples of $4$ are numbers that can be a product of $4$ and another whole number. 

$4 \times \, ? = 10$

There is no whole number that can be multiplied by $4$ to get a product of $10$.  So, $10$ is not a multiple of $4$.
Since we are adding $6$, which is greater than $0$, every number in the pattern will be greater than the previous number.

The only true statement is:

- Every number in the pattern is greater than the previous number.

choices.content: Every other number in the pattern is odd.
The pattern includes only multiples of $4$.
Every number in the pattern is greater than the previous number.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following pattern starts with $7$ and lists the first $10$ multiples of $7$.

$7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63, 70$

**Which of the following statements are true?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
[[Ōśā image 1]]

Each number is $\greenD{7 \text{ greater}}$ than the previous number.

Let's see how many $\maroonD{\text{odd numbers}}$ and $\blueD{\text{even numbers}}$ are in the pattern.

$ \maroonD7, \blueD{14}, \maroonD{21}, \blueD{28},  \maroonD{35}, \blueD{42},  \maroonD{49}, \blueD{56},  \maroonD{63}, \blueD{70}$

There are $\maroonD{5 \text{ odd numbers}}$ and $\blueD{5\text{ even numbers}}$.

The pattern is multiples of $\purpleD7$.  

$\purpleD7$ is a factor of all of its multiples.  

For example, $\purpleD7$ is a factor of $\goldD{56}$, because $\purpleD7 \times 8 = \goldD{56}$.

The true statements are:

- Each number is $7$ greater than the previous number.   

- $7$ is a factor of every number.

choices.content: Each number is $7$ greater than the previous number.
$7$ is a factor of every number.
The pattern has more odd numbers than even numbers.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following pattern starts with $8$ and uses the rule *add $13$ to the previous term.*

$8, 21, 34, 47, 60, \ldots$

**Which of the following statements are true about the eighth term of the pattern?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's figure out the $\maroonD{8^{\text{th}}}$ term in the pattern  by $\greenD{\text{adding 13}}$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

The $\maroonD{8^{\text{th}}}$ term in the pattern is $\maroonD{99}$.
$\blueD9 \times 11 = \maroonD{99}$, so $\maroonD{99}$ is a multiple of $\blueD9$.

$\maroonD{99}$ is *less than* $100$.

$\maroonD{99}$ is an odd number.

The true statements are:

- It is a multiple of $9$.  

- It is an odd number.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following pattern starts with $3$ and uses the rule *add $11$ to the previous term.*

$3, 14, 25, 36, 47, \ldots$

**Which of the following statements are true about the seventh term of the pattern?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints:
Let's figure out the $\blueD{7^{\text{th}}}$ term in the pattern  by $\greenD{\text{adding 11}}$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]


The $\blueD{7^{\text{th}}}$ term in the pattern is $\blueD{69}$.
$\redC3\times 23 = \blueD{69}$, so $\blueD{69}$ is a multiple of $\redC3$.
$\blueD{69}$ is *less than* $100$.
$\blueD{69}$ is an odd number.

The true statements are:

- It is a multiple of $3$.  

- It is less than $100$.
